title: Uncovering Ancient Regulatory DNA in Plant Evolution
summary: A recent study published in Science has identified over 2.3 million conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs) in plants, revealing ancient regulatory DNA switches that date back more than 400 million years. Researchers from Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and international collaborators used a new computational tool called Conservatory to analyze 314 plant genomes from 284 species. These CNSs are regulatory DNA elements that control gene expression, and their discovery challenges previous beliefs that such conservation might not exist in plants. The study found that some CNSs originated before flowering plants diverged from non-flowering ancestors. By comparing gene clusters across hundreds of genomes, scientists detected these hidden sequences, which earlier methods had missed. The research also outlined three key evolutionary patterns: CNS order along chromosomes stays consistent, they can link to different genes during genome rearrangements, and they often persist after gene duplication. This work provides a comprehensive atlas for plant biology, offering insights into how regulatory DNA evolves and can be applied to crop science for improving agricultural species.

background_read: Regulatory DNA consists of sequences that control gene activity without coding for proteins. In plants, understanding how these elements evolve over deep time has been a challenge due to limited conservation compared to animals. Advances in genetics and computational tools now allow scientists to compare hundreds of genomes to uncover hidden patterns. This study addresses a long-standing puzzle in biology by showing that regulatory DNA in plants is more conserved than previously thought, with implications for evolutionary biology and agriculture.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The study identified over 2.3 million conserved non-coding sequences in plants, revealing ancient regulatory DNA switches dating back 400 million years, and outlined three evolutionary patterns for their conservation. Purpose: To resolve whether regulatory DNA is conserved in plants over long periods and provide insights into plant evolution and crop improvement. Evidence Evaluation: High-quality evidence from computational analysis of 314 plant genomes using the Conservatory tool, with genetic editing confirming functional importance. Author Credibility: Researchers from reputable institutions like Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and international collaborators, with expertise in genetics and plant biology. Methodology: Comparative genomics approach analyzing gene clusters across species, combined with new computational tools to detect conserved elements missed by earlier methods.

perspectives.description: This research advances understanding of how regulatory DNA evolves in plants, showing conservation patterns similar to animals and offering new insights into deep time evolutionary processes.
